concept.headline: Every value in Rust has a precise type known at compile time.
concept.explanation: Rust's type system is static — the compiler knows the exact type of every value before the program runs. Scalar types represent a single value: integers (i32, u8, …), floats (f32, f64), booleans, and chars. Compound types group values: tuples hold a fixed number of mixed-type items; arrays hold a fixed number of same-type items.
keyPoints: Default integer type is i32; default float is f64
bool values are exactly true or false — not 0/1
char is a Unicode scalar (4 bytes) — not a byte
Tuple elements accessed with .0, .1, … — no variable index
Array size is part of the type: [i32; 3] ≠ [i32; 4]

demo.intro: Watch Rust allocate four scalar variables and one tuple, then print them all.

explanation.why: i32 is a signed 32-bit integer — suitable for whole numbers in the range –2,147,483,648 to 2,147,483,647
explanation.intent: Store a whole number representing a person's age
explanation.misconception: i32 and u32 have the same size (4 bytes) but u32 cannot hold negatives — choose based on whether negative values are possible

explanation.why: f64 is a 64-bit floating-point number — Rust's default float, providing ~15 decimal digits of precision
explanation.intent: Store a decimal value with high precision
explanation.misconception: Floats are not exact — 3.14 is stored as the closest representable IEEE 754 value, which is slightly off from the true decimal

explanation.why: bool holds exactly two values: true or false. Rust does not coerce integers to bool.
explanation.intent: Store a flag representing an on/off or yes/no state
explanation.misconception: Unlike C, you cannot use 0 or 1 in place of false/true in Rust — if conditions must be bool

explanation.why: char stores a single Unicode scalar value — it can hold letters, digits, emoji, or any Unicode codepoint
explanation.intent: Store a single character for display or comparison
explanation.misconception: A char in Rust is 4 bytes (Unicode), not 1 byte like in C. Use &str or String for text.

explanation.why: Tuples group different types into a single compound value. Type is inferred as (i32, f64) from the elements.
explanation.intent: Bundle age and pi together so both can be passed or returned as one unit
explanation.misconception: Tuples have a fixed length — you cannot push items into a tuple at runtime like you can with a Vec

explanation.why: println! formats each value using its Display implementation
explanation.intent: Verify the stored values match what was assigned
explanation.misconception: {} uses Display formatting; {:?} uses Debug formatting — tuples and arrays require {:?}

explanation.what: Printing the tuple with debug format
explanation.why: Tuples do not implement Display, so {:?} (Debug) is required
explanation.intent: Show the full tuple structure in the console
explanation.misconception: Using {} instead of {:?} for a tuple would cause a compile error — tuples lack a Display impl by default

prediction.explanation: t.0 is 10 (i32), t.1 is 'z' (char — printed without quotes), t.2 is false (bool). {} formats each naturally: 10 z false.

explanation.afterDemo: You saw four scalar slots and one tuple created in memory. Scalars are independent; the tuple groups two of them under a single name. The Debug format {:?} is necessary for compound types like tuples and arrays because they have no default Display.
keyTakeaways: Rust's scalar types are i-integers, f-floats, bool, and char.
Tuple elements are accessed with .0, .1, … — compile-time indices only.
Arrays have a fixed size that is part of the type; use Vec<T> for growable lists.

practice.prompt: Declare an array of three i32 values: 10, 20, 30. Print the second element (index 1).

feedback.successMessage: Correct! You declared a fixed-size array and accessed an element by index.
retryHints: Index with nums[1] — arrays are zero-indexed, so index 1 is the second element.
Expected output is exactly 20 (no trailing spaces).

summary.title: Data Types — Complete
takeaways: Rust's type system is static — every binding has a known type at compile time.
Scalar types: integers, floats, bool, char.
Compound types: tuples (mixed, fixed-length) and arrays (same type, fixed-length).
